It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: The board changed with 9 cells changed after pressing RIGHT. Let me compare the before and after frames to see exactly what changed.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Compare before and after to see what RIGHT did
before = last_transition.before_frame
after = last_transition.after_frame

before_seg = before.segmentation['nodes']
after_seg = after.segmentation['nodes']

# Find nodes that differ between before and after
# Compare by color, hash, pixel count, boundary positions
before_map = {}
for n in before_seg:
    before_map[n['id']] = n

after_map = {}
for n in after_seg:
    after_map[n['id']] = n

# Check for boundary changes
print("Nodes with changed boundaries:")
for nid in before_map:
    if nid in after_map:
        b_before = before_map[nid]['boundary']
        b_after = after_map[nid]['boundary']
        if b_before != b_after:
            print(f"  Node {nid}: color={before_map[nid]['color']}, before={b_before}, after={b_after}")

# Check for new/removed nodes
before_ids = set(before_map.keys())
after_ids = set(after_map.keys())
print(f"\nNew nodes: {after_ids - before_ids}")
print(f"Removed nodes: {before_ids - after_ids}")

# Check for color changes
print("\nNodes with color changes:")
for nid in before_ids & after_ids:
    if before_map[nid]['color'] != after_map[nid]['color']:
        print(f"  Node {nid}: {before_map[nid]['color']} -> {after_map[nid]['color']}")

# Check pixel count changes
print("\nNodes with pixel count changes:")
for nid in before_ids & after_ids:
    if before_map[nid]['pixels'] != after_map[nid]['pixels']:
        print(f"  Node {nid}: {before_map[nid]['pixels']} -> {after_map[nid]['pixels']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
So pressing RIGHT moved the light green (N) 2x2 square from position (40,10)-(41,11) to (40,12)-(41,13) - it moved right by 2 cells inside the gray box. The gray box boundary also changed to accommodate this. Also some bottom border changed slightly.
So the movement keys control one of the objects on the board. The light green dot moved right. Let me check which object is the "active" one - it seems like the light green dot (node 34) is controlled by the arrow keys.
Let me try to understand the goal better. The right panel has two target shapes (red and blue with white borders). The left panel has various pieces. Maybe I need to move the green dot to collect or activate things?
Let me try more movements to understand the mechanics better.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# The green dot (N) moved right. Let me try moving it around more to understand
# First undo by going LEFT, then try UP
action(['LEFT'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
